title: Python Foundations
tagline: Write real Python code from your very first line.

lessons.title: Run Your First Python Code

accomplishments: Install Python on your computer
Write your first line of code
See it work in real-time

do.title: Install Python and run your first code
steps: Click the big yellow 'Download Python' button
IMPORTANT: Check the box that says 'Add Python to PATH' before clicking Install
Click 'Install Now' and wait for installation (2-3 minutes)
Open Visual Studio Code (or download it free from code.visualstudio.com)
Name it hello.py (the .py tells your computer it's Python code)
do.code: print("Hello, CodeHerWay! I just ran my first Python code")
do.result: Your computer just read your Python code and displayed your message!
do.proofRequired: your code AND the output message in the terminal

concepts.name: Python (programming language)
concepts.definition: A programming language - a way to give instructions to your computer. Unlike HTML (which structures content) or CSS (which styles content), Python can perform actions, make decisions, and process data.
concepts.analogy: If HTML is the blueprint of a house and CSS is the paint, Python is the electricity that makes things actually work.

concepts.name: The print() function
concepts.definition: Tells Python to display something on the screen. Whatever you put inside the parentheses () will appear in the terminal.
concepts.analogy: print() doesn't print to a printer. It 'prints' (displays) text on your screen.

concepts.name: The .py file extension
concepts.definition: Tells your computer 'this is Python code'. Just like .html tells your computer 'this is a webpage' and .docx means 'this is a Word document'.
concepts.analogy: A label on a container - .py tells your computer how to open and run the file.

understand.keyTakeaway: Python reads your code top to bottom and executes (runs) each instruction.

build.goal: Personalize the message with your actual name and something you're excited to learn

codeComparison.old: print("Hello, CodeHerWay! I just ran my first Python code")
codeComparison.new: print("Hello! My name is YOUR_NAME and I'm learning Python to build AI apps!")

build.hint:
Try adding more lines! Each print() creates a new line:
print("Hello! My name is Jenna")
print("I'm learning Python")
print("This is my first lesson and I'm already running code!")

challenge.title: Make a Motivational Message Generator
challenge.mission: Create a Python file that displays a three-part motivational message when you run it.
requirements: Use 3 separate print() statements
First line: Your name + a greeting
Second line: What you're learning
Third line: Why it matters to you
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add emojis to each line to make it more fun! (Yes, Python supports emojis)

capabilities: Write code using the print() function
Run Python code and see results

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about variables - how to store and reuse information in your code (so you don't have to type the same thing over and over).

lessons.devFession: I spent 45 minutes trying to install Python the first time because I forgot to check 'Add to PATH'. Then I blamed my computer. It was me the whole time.

lessons.title: Store Information with Variables

accomplishments: Store your name in code (so you can reuse it)
Create a 'user profile' with multiple pieces of information
Understand why variables are the foundation of ALL programming

do.title: Create your first variable
steps: Create a new file: File -> New File
Save it as variables.py on your Desktop
Run it: Right-click -> Run Python File in Terminal
Watch what happens - your name appears 3 times!
do.result: You stored 'Jenna' in a container called name, then displayed it 3 times without typing it 3 times. That's the power of variables!
do.proofRequired: your code AND the terminal with your name repeated 3 times

concepts.name: Variables
concepts.definition: A labeled container that stores information. Once you store something, you can reuse it over and over without retyping it.
concepts.analogy: A labeled box in your house. You put your keys in a box labeled 'keys', so you always know where to find them. In code, you put 'Jenna' in a variable called name, so you can always access it.

concepts.name: The equals sign (=)
concepts.definition: Stores a value in a variable. In Python, = doesn't mean 'equals' like in math. It means 'store this value in this variable.'
concepts.analogy: name = "Jenna" isn't asking 'does name equal Jenna?' - it's saying 'put Jenna in the name container.'

concepts.name: Data Types
concepts.definition: Different kinds of data that Python can work with: Strings (text in quotes), Integers (whole numbers without quotes), and Booleans (True/False).
concepts.analogy: Like different containers for different things - you wouldn't put soup in a paper bag. Each type of data needs to be handled in the right way.

concepts.name: Variable naming rules
concepts.definition: Variables must start with a letter or underscore, can contain letters/numbers/underscores, no spaces allowed, and are case sensitive (Name and name are different).
concepts.analogy: Like naming files on your computer - there are rules about what characters you can use.

understand.keyTakeaway: Variables let you write code once and reuse it infinitely.

build.goal: Create multiple variables to store different pieces of information about yourself

codeComparison.new:
name = "Jenna"
age = 32
city = "Chicago"
is_learning_python = True

print("Name:", name)
print("Age:", age)
print("City:", city)
print("Learning Python:", is_learning_python)

build.hint: When you print text + a variable, use a comma: print("Label:", variable_name)

challenge.title: Build a 'Two Truths and a Lie' Profile
challenge.mission: Create a fun profile with 3 facts about yourself, but make one of them false!
requirements: Create 6 variables: name, age, city, fact_1, fact_2, fact_3
Make all 3 facts strings (in quotes)
Print your name, then the 3 facts
One fact should be a lie (be creative!)
challenge.starterCode:
name = "..."
age = ...
city = "..."

fact_1 = "..."
fact_2 = "..."
fact_3 = "..."

print("About", name)
print("Fact 1:", fact_1)
# Add fact 2 and 3
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a 4th variable called the_lie that stores which number is false, then print it at the end as a reveal!

capabilities: Use different data types (strings, integers, booleans)
Combine text and variables in print statements

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about operators - how to do math, compare values, and make decisions in your code.

lessons.devFession: I once spent 20 minutes debugging because I named a variable 'Class' (capital C) and kept referencing 'class' (lowercase). Python saw them as two completely different things.

lessons.title: Operators & Math

accomplishments: Do math with Python
Compare values to get True/False answers
Build a simple calculator

do.title: Use Python as a calculator
steps: Run it and see the results
do.code:
# Basic math
print(10 + 3)    # Addition: 13
print(10 - 3)    # Subtraction: 7
print(10 * 3)    # Multiplication: 30
print(10 / 3)    # Division: 3.333...
print(10 ** 2)   # Power: 100
print(10 % 3)    # Remainder: 1

# Comparison (gives True or False)
print(10 > 5)    # True
print(10 == 10)  # True (== means 'is equal to?')
print(10 != 5)   # True (!= means 'is NOT equal to?')
do.result: Python can do math AND compare values. The comparison operators return True or False - you'll use these everywhere in the next lesson!
do.proofRequired: your code and the terminal output showing all the results

concepts.name: Arithmetic operators
concepts.definition: Symbols that perform math: + (add), - (subtract), * (multiply), / (divide), ** (power), % (remainder/modulo).
concepts.analogy: The same math you already know, just written slightly differently. The * for multiply and ** for power are the only new ones.

concepts.name: Comparison operators
concepts.definition: Symbols that compare two values and return True or False: == (equal?), != (not equal?), > (greater?), < (less?), >= (greater or equal?), <= (less or equal?).
concepts.analogy: Like a judge comparing two things and giving a yes/no verdict.

concepts.name: Logical operators
concepts.definition: Words that combine True/False values: 'and' (both must be true), 'or' (at least one must be true), 'not' (flips True to False).
concepts.analogy: Like combining rules: 'You can enter IF you're 21 AND you have ID' vs 'You can enter IF you're a member OR you have a ticket.'

understand.keyTakeaway: Operators let you calculate values and compare them. Comparison results (True/False) are the foundation of decision-making in code.

build.goal: Build a tip calculator using variables and operators

codeComparison.new:
bill = 48.50
tip_percent = 20

tip = bill * (tip_percent / 100)
total = bill + tip

print("Bill: $" + str(bill))
print("Tip (" + str(tip_percent) + "%): $" + str(tip))
print("Total: $" + str(total))

build.hint: Use str() to convert numbers to text when combining with + in print statements.

challenge.title: Build a Grade Calculator
challenge.mission: Create a program that takes 3 test scores, calculates the average, and determines if the student passed.
requirements: Store 3 test scores in variables
Calculate the average
Determine if average >= 70 (passing)
Print the average and pass/fail status
challenge.starterCode:
score_1 = 85
score_2 = 72
score_3 = 90

# Calculate average
average = ___

# Check if passing
passing = average >= 70

print("Average:", average)
print("Passing:", passing)
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a letter grade: 90+ = A, 80+ = B, 70+ = C, 60+ = D, below 60 = F (hint: you'll need if/elif from the next lesson!)

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about if statements - how to make your code choose different paths based on conditions (using those True/False comparisons you just learned!).

lessons.devFession: I used = instead of == to compare values for an entire week before someone told me the difference. = stores a value. == checks if two values are equal. I still mess this up sometimes.

lessons.title: Make Decisions with If Statements

accomplishments: Make your code choose different paths
Build a program that responds differently based on input
Create your first interactive decision system
Understand how every app makes choices

do.title: Write code that makes a choice
steps: Create a new file in VS Code
You'll see 'You are an adult!' appear!
Now change age to 15 and run again - notice nothing prints!
do.code:
age = 20

if age >= 18:
    print("You are an adult!")

# Now try changing age to 15 and run again.
# Nothing prints! The code was skipped.
do.result: Your code just made a decision. When age was 20, the condition was True, so it ran. When age was 15, the condition was False, so it skipped that code entirely.
do.proofRequired: your code with age = 15 and NO output in the terminal

concepts.name: If statements
concepts.definition: How code makes decisions. IF a condition is True, run this code. IF it's False, skip it.
concepts.analogy: A bouncer at a club checking IDs. IF you're 21+, you enter. IF not, you don't. The bouncer doesn't let everyone in - they make a decision based on a rule.

concepts.name: Indentation (CRITICAL)
concepts.definition: The code INSIDE the if statement MUST be indented (4 spaces or 1 tab). Python uses indentation to know what's inside vs outside the if statement.
concepts.analogy: Like nesting - the indented code 'belongs to' the if statement above it. No indentation = error.

concepts.name: Else statement
concepts.definition: If the condition is False, do this instead. A fork in the road - your code always takes one path.
concepts.analogy: A fork in the road. You MUST go one way or the other. You can't skip both.

concepts.name: Elif (else if)
concepts.definition: If the first condition was False, check this one. Like a multiple choice quiz - Python checks each option in order and stops at the first True one.
concepts.analogy: A multiple choice quiz. Python checks the first answer. If wrong, checks the second. Else = 'none of the above.'

understand.keyTakeaway: If statements let your code choose different paths based on conditions. Every app you use makes thousands of decisions per second using this exact concept.

build.goal: Build a multi-condition age checker with elif
build.hint: Try running with different ages: 10, 16, 45, 70 - watch how it picks different messages!

challenge.title: Build a Movie Ticket Pricer
challenge.mission: Create a program that calculates movie ticket prices based on age and other factors.
requirements: Create variables: age, is_student, day_of_week
Children under 12: $8
Students (with ID): $10
Seniors 65+: $9
Regular adults: $15
Tuesday discount: Take $2 off any price
Print the final price with a message
challenge.starterCode:
age = 25
is_student = False
day_of_week = "Tuesday"

# Your price logic here
if age < 12:
    price = 8
    category = "Child"
# Add more conditions...

# Tuesday discount
if day_of_week == "Tuesday":
    price = price - 2

print("Category:", category)
print("Final price: $" + str(price))
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a weekend surcharge! If it's Saturday or Sunday, add $3 to the price.

capabilities: Write if statements to make decisions
Use else to handle alternative outcomes
Combine logical operators inside if statements

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about loops - how to make your code repeat actions automatically (without copy/pasting the same code 100 times).

lessons.devFession: I once wrote 47 separate if statements instead of using elif. My code worked but my coworker literally laughed out loud during code review. Now I always use elif.

lessons.title: Automate with For Loops

accomplishments: Make Python repeat tasks automatically
Print your name 100 times without typing it 100 times
Loop through lists of data
Build a countdown timer

do.title: Print 'Hello' 5 times without typing it 5 times
do.result: You just told Python: 'Do this thing 5 times.' And it did. Instantly. Try range(100) and watch it go!
do.proofRequired: your code with range(100) and the terminal full of repeated messages

concepts.name: For loops
concepts.definition: Repeats code a specific number of times. Write it once, tell Python how many times to run it.
concepts.analogy: Setting a timer for jumping jacks. 'Do 20 jumping jacks' is one instruction, not 20 separate ones.

concepts.name: The range() function
concepts.definition: Creates a sequence of numbers. range(5) gives 0,1,2,3,4. range(1,6) gives 1,2,3,4,5. range(10,0,-1) counts backwards.
concepts.analogy: A number generator - you tell it where to start, stop, and how to count.

concepts.name: The loop variable (i)
concepts.definition: Holds the current number in each iteration. First loop: i=0. Second: i=1. And so on.
concepts.analogy: A page counter in a book - it tracks which page you're currently on.

concepts.name: Indentation (again!)
concepts.definition: Code inside the loop MUST be indented. Indented = repeats. Not indented = runs once after the loop.
concepts.analogy: Same rule as if statements - Python uses indentation to know what's inside.

concepts.name: The Accumulator Pattern
concepts.definition: Start with an initial value (like total=0), add to it each loop, use the final result. Used for totals, averages, counting.
concepts.analogy: A piggy bank. Start with $0, add money each day, count the total at the end.

understand.keyTakeaway: Loops let you automate repetitive tasks with a single instruction. This is the entire reason programming exists.

build.goal: Build a countdown timer and a number accumulator

codeComparison.old:
for i in range(5):
    print("Hello, CodeHerWay!")
codeComparison.new:
# Countdown
for i in range(10, 0, -1):
    print(i)
print("Blastoff!")

# Accumulator: add 1 through 10
total = 0
for i in range(1, 11):
    total += i
    print("Added", i, "- Total:", total)

build.hint: range(10, 0, -1) means: start at 10, stop before 0, count by -1 (backwards).

challenge.title: Build a Multiplication Table Generator
challenge.mission: Create a program that prints the multiplication table for any number.
requirements: Store the number in a variable
Use a loop to multiply by 1 through 10
Print each result in a readable format
Calculate and display the sum of all results
challenge.starterCode:
number = 7
total = 0

for i in range(1, 11):
    result = ___
    print(___)
    total += ___

print("Sum of all results:", total)
challenge.bonusChallenge: Make it generate multiplication tables for multiple numbers! Loop through 1-5 and print each table.

capabilities: Write for loops to repeat code
Use range() with start, stop, and step

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about lists - how to store multiple pieces of data together and loop through them (which is where loops get REALLY powerful).

lessons.devFession: I once copy-pasted the same print statement 50 times instead of using a loop. When my bootcamp instructor saw my code, she just said 'Oh honey, no.' and taught me for loops.

lessons.title: Organize Data with Lists

accomplishments: Store multiple pieces of data in a single variable
Access individual items from a list
Add, remove, and modify list items
Loop through lists to process data
Build a real task manager

do.title: Create your first list
steps: Run it and see the list printed
Then access individual items by index
do.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "mango"]

print(fruits)       # Whole list
print(fruits[0])    # First item: apple
print(fruits[1])    # Second item: banana
print(fruits[-1])   # Last item: mango
do.result: You stored 4 pieces of data in a SINGLE variable. Instead of fruit1, fruit2, fruit3, fruit4, you grouped them all together!
do.proofRequired: your list and printing the first and last items

concepts.name: Lists
concepts.definition: A collection of items stored in a single variable, kept in order. Created with square brackets [].
concepts.analogy: A shopping list on paper. All items on ONE list, numbered in order.

concepts.name: Index positions (start at 0!)
concepts.definition: Python counts list positions from 0, not 1. First item is [0], second is [1]. Negative numbers count from the end: [-1] is the last item.
concepts.analogy: Apartment numbers starting from 0 instead of 1. Confusing at first, but every programming language works this way.

concepts.name: List methods
concepts.definition: append() adds to the end, remove() removes a specific item, insert() adds at a position, pop() removes and returns the last item, len() counts items.
concepts.analogy: Tools in a toolbox - each one modifies your list in a specific way.

concepts.name: Looping through lists
concepts.definition: for item in my_list: processes every item automatically. This is the most common pattern in programming.
concepts.analogy: A conveyor belt - each item passes through your code one at a time.

concepts.name: The 'in' keyword
concepts.definition: Checks if something exists in a list: if 'eggs' in shopping_list. Returns True or False.
concepts.analogy: Ctrl+F for lists - searches through every item to find a match.

understand.keyTakeaway: Lists store multiple items in order. Access by index (starting from 0). Loop through them to process every item automatically.

build.goal: Modify your list and loop through it with conditions

codeComparison.new:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
fruits.append("mango")
fruits[1] = "blueberry"
fruits.remove("cherry")

for fruit in fruits:
    print("I love", fruit + "s!")

print("Total fruits:", len(fruits))

build.hint: len(my_list) gives you the count. If a list has 5 items, len() returns 5, but the last index is 4 (because we start at 0)!

challenge.title: Build a Task Manager
challenge.mission: Create a task manager that stores tasks, marks them complete, and shows progress.
requirements: Create a list of 5 tasks
Create a parallel list tracking completion (True/False)
Loop through and print incomplete tasks only
Mark a task as complete and show the update
Show count of total and completed tasks with percentage
challenge.starterCode:
tasks = ["Learn Python", "Build a project", "Practice loops", "Master lists", "Celebrate!"]
completed = [False, False, False, False, False]

# Show incomplete tasks
for i in range(len(tasks)):
    if not completed[i]:
        print(i + 1, "-", tasks[i])

# Mark task 0 complete
completed[0] = True

# Count progress
total = len(tasks)
done = 0
for status in completed:
    if status:
        done += 1
print("Progress:", done, "/", total)
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add the ability to add new tasks using append() on both lists!

capabilities: Create lists to store multiple items

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about functions - how to package your code into reusable blocks that you can call anytime.

lessons.devFession: I once created 200 separate variables (item1, item2, item3...) instead of using a list. My mentor looked at my code and said 'I'm going to teach you something that will change your life.' She was right.

lessons.title: Build Reusable Code with Functions

accomplishments: Package code into reusable blocks
Create functions that accept inputs and return outputs
Stop repeating yourself in code
Build a library of your own tools

do.title: Create your first function
steps: Now call greet() three more times
do.code:
def greet():
    print("Hello, CodeHerWay!")
    print("Welcome to functions!")

# Call it!
greet()
greet()
greet()
do.result: You wrote the code ONCE inside the function, then ran it 3 times by calling its name. Write once, use forever!
do.proofRequired: your function being called at least 3 times with output

concepts.name: Functions
concepts.definition: A named block of code you can run anytime by calling its name. Write once, reuse forever.
concepts.analogy: A recipe. You write it once, then cook that dish anytime. You don't rewrite the recipe each time.

concepts.name: The def keyword
concepts.definition: Tells Python 'here comes a reusable block of code.' Always followed by the function name, parentheses, and a colon.
concepts.analogy: Like labeling a container - def gives your code block a name so you can find it later.

concepts.name: Parameters and Arguments
concepts.definition: Parameters are input slots in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values you pass when calling. def greet(name) has a parameter; greet('Jenna') passes an argument.
concepts.analogy: A machine with input slots (parameters). You feed in materials (arguments), it processes them.

concepts.name: The return statement
concepts.definition: Sends a value back to whoever called the function. Without return, a function does its work but gives nothing back.
concepts.analogy: Sending a package. Data goes IN (parameters), function processes it, result comes BACK (return).

concepts.name: Print vs Return
concepts.definition: print() shows text on screen but returns None. return gives back a value you can store and reuse. Use return when you need the result elsewhere.
concepts.analogy: print = showing someone a number. return = handing them the number so they can use it.

understand.keyTakeaway: Functions let you write code once and reuse it anywhere. Parameters make them flexible. Return makes their results usable.

build.goal: Build a calculator with multiple functions that work together
build.hint: Always check for edge cases! Like dividing by zero.

challenge.title: Build a Grade Calculator System
challenge.mission: Create a complete grading system using multiple functions that work together.
requirements: Function to calculate average from a list of scores
Function to convert average to letter grade (A, B, C, D, F)
Function to determine if student passed (C or better)
Function to generate a full report card
Process multiple students using loops
Return and print results for each student
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add weighted grades (homework 40%, tests 60%) and class-wide statistics.

capabilities: Build complex systems from small functions
Combine functions, loops, lists, and if statements

bridge.preview: You've completed Module 1: Foundations! In Module 2, you'll learn dictionaries, file handling, error handling, and working with external data.

lessons.devFession: I once copied the same 15 lines of code into 8 different places in my program. When I needed to fix a bug, I had to fix it 8 times. Functions exist so you never have to do that.
